explicit_text: My mother, Maria Teresa Altamirano, was a housewife. She raised me and my siblings in South Texas, specifically around Brownsville. Growing up, she was a central figure in our family, supporting us while my father worked as an electrician. Her role shaped much of my early life and values.

explicit_text: My father, Miguel Angel Altamirano, was an electrician by trade. He worked hard to provide for our family in South Texas, and I am the senior among my siblings. His dedication to his work and family influenced me greatly as I grew up in the Lower Rio Grande Valley. He was a steady presence while I was deciding my own path after high school.

explicit_text: Jorge Alberto Altamirano is my brother, born after my sister Maria Teresa and before Angelica and Javier. Although I was the first in the family to join the military, Jorge was always a part of my support system back home. Our family ties remain strong despite the different paths we took.

explicit_text: I voluntarily enlisted in the United States Marine Corps right after graduating from Hanna High School in 1976. Despite the general discontent about the military following the Vietnam War, I was drawn to the Marines after a recruiter visit to my school. The Marine Corps exposed me to the diversity of the country, which was quite different from my upbringing in South Texas. My service was a defining chapter in my life, shaping my views and experiences.

explicit_text: I attended Hanna High School in South Texas and graduated in 1976. It was there that military recruiters, including Staff Sergeant Watson, came to visit, which motivated me to enlist in the Marine Corps. The school environment was a place where I first considered my future beyond the local community. Hanna High School played a crucial role in my decision to join the military.

explicit_text: The Vietnam War had just ended a few years before I graduated high school in 1976, and the public attitude towards the military was largely negative. There was a lot of resentment and dissatisfaction with the government's involvement in such a distant conflict. This atmosphere made it difficult for recruiters like Staff Sergeant Watson to encourage enlistment, but I chose to join the Marine Corps regardless. The war's aftermath shaped the environment in which I made my decision.

explicit_text: Staff Sergeant Watson was the military recruiter who visited my high school in 1976. He was an Anglo, tall, and looked like a poster Marine, which made a strong impression on me. Despite the general discontent with the military after Vietnam, he was able to inspire me to enlist in the Marine Corps. His visit was a pivotal moment that influenced my decision to serve.

explicit_text: I was raised in South Texas, specifically in the Lower Rio Grande Valley area including Brownsville and McAllen. Growing up there, I experienced a community that was predominantly Latino with very little diversity. This background made my later exposure to the broader diversity of the United States Marine Corps quite eye-opening. South Texas shaped my early life and cultural perspective before I enlisted in 1976.

My upbringing in Brownsville was typical of the region, and it was from there that I went to Hanna High School and later enlisted in the Marine Corps in 1976. Brownsville remains an important part of my identity.

This contrast became very clear during my military service.
